segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: They found her by the half-fallen chapel arch, alone. The chapel had taken smoke damage on the south wall; one window was gone, and the pale morning light came through the gap in a way that made the remaining stone look older than it was.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: She stood with her fingers resting on the arch, not in prayer -- she'd have been the first to say so -- but in the way of someone reading a language they used to be fluent in and were not sure they still spoke.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The stone was cool under her fingertips.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: She could feel the mason's work -- the precision of the cuts, the way each block had been fitted to its neighbor with the care of someone who believed that straight lines and right angles were their own form of worship.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Once, she'd felt the divine in stones like these. Now she felt the geometry and the intent, and neither of those were gods. They were craft. They were beautiful. But they weren't the voice she'd spent fifteen years following.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: She heard them approach and didn't turn.

segments.speaker: vex
segments.text: "I've been trying to find a way to say this that isn't a failure,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: she said.

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "I haven't found one. So I'll just say it."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: She turned then. Her face was composed -- the particular composure of someone who'd made a decision and was standing behind it, not because it was easy but because it was the only honest thing left.

segments.speaker: vex
segments.text: "My light failed,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: she said.

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "You've seen it. It may return -- I don't know -- but right now I can't pray with any certainty that someone is listening. And I can't lead you while I don't know whose voice I'm following, if any."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: She let that sit. It deserved the space.

segments.speaker: vex
segments.text: "That would be dishonest. And dishonest faith is worse than no faith at all -- it gets people killed on promises you can't keep."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The silence after that was the kind that asks not to be filled quickly.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Caelin remembered the forked whisper -- Shield the bearer. Offer the bearer.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He'd never heard it himself, but he'd seen its effect on Serana's face: the particular horror of someone who'd discovered that the voice they trusted most might be two things wearing one name. He didn't ask about it now.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: She'd tell them when she was ready, or she wouldn't, and either way it was hers.

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "Where will you go?"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: he asked.

segments.speaker: vex
segments.text: "East. There's a place -- an old house of retreat, not affiliated with any order -- where they take people who are having difficulty with questions like mine."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: A pause.

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "People who have lost the signal and are trying to find it again. Or learning to live without it."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: She looked at the gap in the chapel wall, where the morning light poured through the absence like a answer to a question nobody had asked.

segments.speaker: vex
segments.text: "I don't know which of those I'm doing yet."

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "How long?"

segments.speaker: vex
segments.text: "I don't know that either."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: She almost smiled.

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "Which is, in itself, a kind of answer."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: She unrolled a strip of plain linen inked with a knotwork sigil -- old work, careful, the lines laid down with a steady hand that had not trembled.

segments.speaker: vex
segments.text: "A vigil-knot. No goddess required -- just breath and intention. Tie it round a wrist, speak the count of nine slowly, and it holds a line against panic for about an hour. Not much."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: She almost smiled.

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "Enough, sometimes."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: She pressed one into Caelin's hand, then set three more on the stone ledge of the arch.

segments.speaker: vex
segments.text: "For whoever needs them. They'll keep."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Thornik took one without being asked, wrapping it around his thick wrist like a bracelet.

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "Panic and I are old friends,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: he said.

segments.speaker: vex
segments.text: "We have an arrangement. But backup never hurts."

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "If I'm meant to come back, I will,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Serana said.

segments.speaker: vex
segments.text: "And if not -- "

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: her eyes moved to each of them in turn, settling last on Caelin

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: " -- you already know how to stand. You've been doing it since the mountain. You just didn't notice because I was nearby."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Vex had been quiet the whole time, which was unusual enough to notice. She unfolded her arms -- carefully, because of the rib -- and said:

segments.speaker: vex
segments.text: "If your god is taking interviews, tell Her the customer service is abysmal."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The ghost of a smile crossed Serana's face -- real, not performed. The kind that comes when someone gives you exactly the thing you need, and the thing you need turns out to be a joke.

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "I'll pass it along."

segments.speaker: vex
segments.text: "Also tell Her,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Vex continued,

segments.speaker: vex
segments.text: "that you were the best paladin I've met, and I've met four, and three of them were idiots. You were the one who wasn't. So if She's got any sense, She'll pick up the damn prayer and answer it."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Serana's composure cracked, just once, just briefly -- the flash of emotion underneath the armor, the person beneath the title. She blinked once, steadied.

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "Thank you."

segments.speaker: vex
segments.text: "Don't thank me. I'll deny saying it."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Serana picked up her pack. It was lighter than it had been. She'd left the heavy things behind -- and the light things, too. She'd left everything that wasn't her.
